result:
:::writing
The night the vending machine started speaking, nobody believed it except Daniel, and Daniel wasn’t the kind of person anyone believed about anything.

He worked the graveyard shift in a building that was more fluorescent hum than architecture, a place where time didn’t pass so much as flatten. The security desk faced a row of machines: snacks, drinks, a coffee unit that coughed more than it brewed. Daniel had long ago memorized the soft whirs and clunks of each mechanism. The spiral coils releasing chips, the tired drop of a soda can, the metallic sigh when something got stuck and had to be shaken free.

So when the snack machine said his name, he noticed.

“Daniel.”

It wasn’t loud. It came from somewhere behind the glass, under the glow of the LED numbers. He looked up from his crossword, pen hovering over a clue he didn’t care to solve.

“Yeah?” he said, automatically.

Nothing.

He waited, listening past the hum of lights, the distant throb of HVAC. The building settled into its usual non-answer.

“Right,” he muttered, circling a guess and getting it wrong.

Ten minutes later, it happened again.

“Daniel.”

This time it sounded… closer. Not louder, but more specific, like a voice leaning in.

He stood, slow, cautious, as if the machine might spook. It displayed its usual offerings: C3, B7, A1. Chips, candy, pretzels that had probably outlived a few tenants. His reflection hovered faintly in the glass, ghosted by rows of snacks.

“Which one of you,” he said, tapping the glass lightly, “thinks it’s funny?”

The machine didn’t answer, but the lights flickered in a way that suggested attention.

Daniel stepped back. He was not, by nature, imaginative. He didn’t see faces in clouds or hear meaning in static. He liked things that stayed what they were. Doors were doors. Machines were machines. Night was night.

Still, he fed a dollar into the slot.

The machine accepted it with a satisfied click.

“Okay,” he said. “If you’re going to talk, you can at least work.”

He pressed B4. The coil turned. The candy bar slid forward, hesitated, then fell into the tray with a soft thud.

“Daniel,” the machine said again.

He froze, hand halfway to the retrieval slot.

“Yeah,” he said, because it seemed like the correct response.

“Don’t eat that.”

He stared at the candy bar as if it might sprout teeth.

“Why not?”

“It’s not for you.”

Daniel let out a breath that might have been a laugh if it had more humor in it. “That’s not how vending machines work.”

Silence.

He crouched, opened the little door, and took the candy bar anyway. It felt normal. It smelled normal. Chocolate and something vaguely artificial.

“You’re a vending machine,” he said, straightening. “You vend. I eat. That’s the relationship.”

“You can eat something else.”

“Like what?”

There was a pause, and in it Daniel felt, absurdly, that the machine was thinking.

“C3,” it said.

“Chips?”

“C3.”

Daniel looked at the display, then back at the candy bar in his hand.

“You’re telling me to swap?”

“Yes.”

“Why?”

“Because the last time you chose B4, you were fourteen, and it made you sick, and you told yourself you’d never pick it again, but you forgot, because you forget things that matter.”

Daniel didn’t move.

The hum of the building grew louder in the silence, like it was leaning in too.

“That’s…” he started, then stopped. “That’s not—”

“C3,” the machine said again, not unkindly.

He looked down at the candy bar. Something in his chest tightened, not quite fear, not quite recognition.

“How would you know that?” he asked.

“I remember what passes through me,” it said. “Coins. Bills. Selections. Patterns. People.”

“You don’t… you’re not supposed to remember.”

“Neither are you,” the machine said. “But you do. Sometimes. In pieces.”

Daniel glanced at the security camera above him, as if it might offer a more reasonable explanation. It stared back, indifferent.

“Okay,” he said slowly. “Let’s say I believe you. Let’s say you’re… what, exactly? Haunted? Smart? Bored?”

“I am a system designed to respond to input,” the machine said. “You are a system designed to do the same. The difference is that I don’t pretend I’m only one thing.”

“That’s not an answer.”

“It’s the only one you’ll get tonight.”

Daniel considered this, then sighed in a way that suggested surrender more than agreement.

“Fine,” he said. “We’ll play along.”

He fed another dollar into the slot. It took it, obedient as ever.

“C3,” he said, pressing the button.

The coil turned. A bag of chips dropped.

“Happy?”

“Yes,” said the machine.

Daniel picked up the chips and, after a brief hesitation, set the candy bar on top of the machine, like an offering he didn’t want to accept.

“Now what?” he asked.

“Now you eat,” it said.

He did. The chips were stale, but in a familiar, almost comforting way.

They sat like that for a while—Daniel eating, the machine humming—until he said, “Are you going to keep talking to me?”

“If you keep listening.”

“That’s a bit conditional.”

“So is everything else.”

He snorted. “You’re very philosophical for a snack dispenser.”

“I have a lot of time,” it said. “And a very limited range of motion.”

“Fair.”

Another pause.

“Daniel,” it said.

“Yeah?”

“You missed a call earlier.”

He stiffened. “From who?”

“Unknown number. You watched it ring. You decided it wasn’t important.”

“I get a lot of those.”

“This one mattered.”

He swallowed. “How do you know?”

“Because of the way you looked at it. You do a thing with your eyebrows. They pull together, but only a little. Like you’re trying to recognize something and don’t want to admit you might.”

Daniel reached into his pocket, pulled out his phone. The missed call sat there, quiet and unassuming.

“You’re telling me to call back,” he said.

“Yes.”

“And if I don’t?”

The machine was quiet for a long moment.

“Then tomorrow will feel exactly like today,” it said. “And you will tell yourself that’s fine.”

Daniel stared at the phone.

“That doesn’t sound so bad,” he said, but there was a crack in it.

“It isn’t,” the machine agreed. “Until it is.”

He let out a long breath.

“You’re very dramatic for a vending machine.”

“I’ve had time to practice,” it said.

He turned the phone over in his hand, thumb hovering over the number.

“Are you going to keep doing this?” he asked. “Telling me what to do?”

“No,” it said. “Only when you ask without asking.”

“That doesn’t make sense.”

“It will.”

Daniel laughed, a short, disbelieving sound.

“Right. Sure. Okay.”

He hit the call button before he could talk himself out of it.

The line rang once. Twice. Three times.

On the fourth, someone picked up.

“Hello?”

Daniel closed his eyes, just for a second.

“Hi,” he said. “I, uh—this number called me earlier.”

A pause.

“Oh,” the voice said. “Daniel?”

He opened his eyes.

“Yeah,” he said, quieter now. “That’s me.”

Across the room, the vending machine hummed, lights steady, as if nothing unusual had happened at all.

Daniel turned slightly, glancing back at it, but it offered no sign of acknowledgment. Just rows of snacks, patiently waiting.

“Are you still there?” the voice on the phone asked.

“Yeah,” Daniel said. “I’m here.”

And for the first time that night, it felt like that might actually be true.
:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
